title: Accelerating physics-informed neural networks for full waveform inversion using a hybrid quantum-classical finite-basis architecture
authors: Hoang Anh Nguyen, Divakar Vashisth, Ali Tura
abstract: Full waveform inversion (FWI) reconstructs heterogeneous material properties from receiver data but remains computationally demanding. Physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) and their domain-decomposed variants (FBPINNs) offer a mesh-free alternative but face convergence challenges when representing complex velocity fields. We present a hybrid quantum-classical FBPINN for acoustic FWI, bringing together quantum computing and classical machine learning, in which the decomposed wavefield network and the global velocity network are implemented as classical-to-quantum pipelines terminating in parameterized quantum circuits (PQCs). The PQCs are realized as differentiable JAX statevector simulators, enabling end-to-end automatic differentiation through the classical PINN, the quantum circuit, and the physics-informed loss. On a geophysical anomaly benchmark, the quantum hybrid reaches a lower L1 velocity error than the primary classical FBPINN baseline in approximately 8x fewer training iterations, despite using approximately 33% fewer trainable parameters, and it outperforms all 15 classical hyperparameter variants tested. A second benchmark (checkerboard) demonstrates the generality of the inversion pipeline, confirming that the quantum hybrid architecture can recover structured spatial variations beyond the localized anomaly benchmark. Our framework is broadly applicable to wave-based inverse problems beyond geophysics, including medical ultrasound tomography and non-destructive evaluation.
summary: This work accelerates Full Waveform Inversion (FWI) by creating a hybrid quantum-classical neural network. It replaces parts of the classical network with Parameterized Quantum Circuits (PQCs) simulated on classical hardware. The resulting model significantly reduces the required training time and parameters compared to purely classical PINN baselines for wave-based inverse problems.
main_problem: The paper addresses the computational bottleneck of Full Waveform Inversion (FWI) for reconstructing material properties from wave data.
main_result: A hybrid quantum-classical architecture significantly accelerates the inversion process, achieving comparable or better accuracy in approximately 8 times fewer training iterations than the best classical baseline.
method: It proposes integrating Parameterized Quantum Circuits (PQCs) into a Physics-Informed Neural Network (PINN) framework, allowing end-to-end automatic differentiation through the quantum components.
model_or_system: The system models acoustic wave propagation governed by the acoustic wave equation, solved using a domain-decomposed FBPINN structure. The quantum component utilizes a PQC realized via differentiable JAX statevector simulators.
key_observables: L1 velocity error, number of training iterations, and number of trainable parameters.
important_parameters: The number of qubits (e.g., 4 qubits), the depth of the PQC (L layers), and the loss function weights ($\lambda_{data}, \lambda_{PDE}, \dots$).
main_assumptions: The quantum advantage is demonstrated using classical statevector simulation, and the comparison is not strictly parameter-matched across all classical baselines.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the hybrid architecture, convergence curves showing error vs. iterations for both classical and quantum models, and the recovery of structured spatial variations (checkerboard pattern).
paper_structure: The paper introduces FWI and PINNs, details the hybrid quantum-classical FBPINN architecture, presents the methodology for end-to-end differentiation, and validates the approach on two benchmarks: a geophysical anomaly and a checkerboard pattern.
why_interesting: While the primary application is geophysics, the methodology of embedding complex physical PDEs into a quantum-enhanced neural network structure is highly relevant for simulating quantum many-body dynamics or solving time-dependent quantum Hamiltonians where classical simulation struggles.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Schematic of the finite-basis classical-quantum hybrid architecture, combining classical neural networks and quantum circuits with physics-informed constraints. Blue boxes denote classical networks N, orange boxes quantum circuits Q, green circles the predicted fields ϕ and α, and purple nodes the physics- and data-based residuals contributing to Ltotal.

Figure 2: Multilayer PQC Q. Each input feature zi is angle-encoded via an Ry(βi zi) rotation (blue encoding layer), where βi is a trainable scaling parameter. Variational layers of single-qubit rotations Rx, Ry, Rz followed by a linear CNOT ladder are repeated L times (orange block). Each qubit is measured in the Pauli-Z basis (green meters), and the outputs are averaged to yield a scalar in [−1, 1].

Figure 3: Anomaly benchmark inversion and convergence. (a) True velocity model used for the spectral-element forward simulation, with source (red star) and receiver array (green triangles). (b) Initial homogeneous velocity model. (c) Inverted velocity model from the quantum hybrid FBPINN at 65,000 training iterations. (d) L1 velocity error versus training iteration for the classical FBPINN baseline (blue), the quantum hybrid (red markers labeled 65k), and 14 of the 15 classical hyperparameter variants (thin colored lines). Traces begin at different initial L1 values because each velocity (α) network initializes to a different (nominally homogeneous) field; this initial spread reflects...

Figure 4: Checkerboard benchmark inversion. (a) True velocity model: a 3 × 3 checkerboard of alternating ±0.4 km/s perturbations on a 3.0 km/s background, with source (red star) and receiver array (green triangles). (b) Inverted velocity model from the quantum hybrid FBPINN, using the identical network configuration from the anomaly benchmark with only the collocation point density increased.
